Context Worth Knowing

Centralized maintenance roles like this one exist because Cortland manages enough units in a given market to justify dedicated trade specialists rather than asking every maintenance tech to do everything. That's actually good news for someone who takes real pride in painting. You're not the person who also fixes HVAC and unclog drains. You're the person who owns the paint function, and the quality of every make-ready unit reflects that.

What separates a strong candidate here from an average one isn't just speed. It's texture matching. A lot of painters can roll a coat. Fewer can blend a drywall repair into an existing finish so it disappears. If that skill is in your toolkit, it's worth highlighting clearly in your application.

The DC metro multifamily market is competitive, and lease-up timelines are tight. A centralized painter who keeps units moving through the make-ready process without quality shortcuts directly supports occupancy goals. The role connects to real operational outcomes, not just aesthetics.

job.title: Centralized Painter - McLean, VA
job.description:
Painting is a trade where the details really do matter. A drip on a baseboard, a thin spot over a patch, overspray on a outlet cover. Residents notice. Cortland notices. This role exists because they want someone who takes that seriously and can move efficiently through a portfolio of apartment turns without cutting corners to hit a number.

Cortland is one of the larger vertically integrated multifamily operators in the country, founded in 2005 and managing a significant portfolio across multiple markets. The McLean position is a centralized painter role, meaning you're focused specifically on painting work across properties rather than splitting time across a general maintenance scope. That specialization is the point. You're expected to complete at least one full apartment paint per day, or two touch-up units, working with spray equipment, rollers, and brushes depending on what each unit needs. Small drywall repairs and texture matching are part of the job too. The ability to prep and finish a unit cleanly, start to finish, is what this role is built around.

You'll also be responsible for the paint shop itself. Keeping it stocked, clean, and organized. Maintaining spray equipment to manufacturer standards. Logging issues that affect your schedule. It's not glamorous paperwork, but it keeps the operation running and protects the equipment budget. If you've ever inherited a shop where the sprayers were clogged and half the supplies were missing, you understand why this matters.

What you'll bring

  • At least 2 years of interior residential painting experience. Custom home work is a plus.
  • Proficiency with spray equipment, rollers, and brushes, including proper prep for each method.
  • Ability to complete small drywall repairs and match texture.
  • Working knowledge of OSHA guidelines and MSDS regulations for paint and related materials.
  • Comfort working on ladders and scaffolding safely.
  • Valid driver's license with current insurance.
  • Availability for varied shifts, including weekends when needed.
  • High school diploma or equivalent.

One thing worth naming honestly: turn work in a multifamily setting has a pace to it. You're not on a custom home with unlimited time to achieve a perfect finish. You're working to a schedule that ties directly to a unit being lease-ready. Strong candidates in this kind of role know how to maintain quality at speed. They've developed an efficient sequence, they don't have to think twice about equipment setup, and they communicate early if something is going to affect the timeline rather than hoping it works out.

If you're someone who genuinely takes pride in a clean, consistent paint job and wants to work within a structured operation that values that craft, this is a solid fit. The centralized model means you're doing the work you're actually good at, every day, rather than bouncing between tasks that have nothing to do with painting.

job.title: Community Relations Manager
job.description:
What You'll Actually Do

This role sits at the intersection of leasing, operations, and resident experience. On any given day, you might walk vacant units and model homes for make-ready readiness, log a fresh lead into the CRM before following up on a tour from yesterday, and then help troubleshoot a vendor delay with the Community Manager. The work shifts fast. You're expected to hold both a polished, consultative sales presence and a sharp operational eye at the same time.

On the leasing side, you'll manage your pipeline with discipline. That means prompt follow-up, consistent engagement, and closing with confidence using consultative techniques that match solutions to what each prospective resident actually needs. Cortland operates at a premium brand level, so the experience you create during a tour or a phone call carries real weight. Conversion metrics, lead activity, and KPIs aren't background noise here. They're the daily scorecard.

Operationally, you'll support the Community Manager on compliance, inspections, vendor relationships, and curb appeal. You'll conduct move-in and move-out walkthroughs, flag maintenance needs, and monitor for policy issues. You'll also run resident events and manage the community's social media presence, which means you're contributing to both retention and new traffic simultaneously.

What You Bring

  • At least two years of measurable sales success in a short-cycle, high-volume environment: multifamily, hospitality, luxury retail, or a comparable field
  • A proven track record of hitting and exceeding leasing or sales quotas
  • Comfort with CRM systems, property management software, and Microsoft Office
  • Strong written and verbal communication skills, including the ability to handle objections with calm and care
  • Experience with social media for brand storytelling or community engagement
  • High school diploma required; a bachelor's degree is preferred
  • A working knowledge of Fair Housing guidelines and general compliance basics

Context Worth Knowing

The Community Relations Manager title at Cortland is genuinely a dual-track position. You're not purely a leasing consultant, and you're not purely an assistant manager. You operate across both lanes, which makes this role a strong development ground. People who succeed here tend to move into full Community Manager roles with a broader operational foundation than peers who spent those same years in a pure leasing seat. The mix of pipeline accountability, vendor coordination, resident programming, and compliance exposure builds the kind of well-rounded profile that carries real weight when you're ready to run a property independently.

One honest note: Cortland's brand standards run high. The expectation isn't just that you close leases. It's that every touchpoint, from a voicemail to a move-in walkthrough, reflects a premium resident experience. Candidates who thrive here bring genuine attention to detail and don't treat follow-up as optional. If you treat your leasing pipeline the way a good closer treats a sales funnel, and you genuinely enjoy building resident relationships beyond the lease signing, this role fits the way you already work.

job.title: Community Relations Manager - Cortland Arlington
job.description:
This role sits at the intersection of leasing and operations. It's not a pure sales job, and it's not a pure assistant manager job. It's both, which means the right candidate has to be comfortable closing a lease in the afternoon and walking a punch list in the morning.

What the Job Actually Covers

Cortland is hiring a Community Relations Manager for their Arlington, VA property. Day to day, you're splitting time between driving occupancy and keeping the community running to brand standards. On the leasing side, that means working a pipeline with discipline: timely follow-up, CRM accuracy, consultative conversations with prospects, and closing. On the operations side, you're supporting the Community Manager with inspections, make-ready walkthroughs, vendor coordination, and policy compliance including Fair Housing and OSHA requirements.

Resident experience is a real part of the job, not just a line in the description. You're planning events, managing social media presence, handling escalated concerns, and building the kind of relationships that generate referrals. Cortland positions itself as a premium operator, so the expectation is a concierge-level standard across every resident touchpoint.

What Cortland Is Looking For

  • At least 2 years of measurable sales success in a high-volume, short-cycle environment. Multifamily experience is a natural fit, but Cortland also considers candidates from luxury hospitality, premium retail, or similar service-oriented sales roles.
  • Demonstrated ability to hit and exceed leasing goals, conversion targets, and KPIs consistently.
  • Comfort with property management software, CRM platforms, and Microsoft Office.
  • Strong written and verbal communication skills, including the ability to handle objections and difficult resident situations professionally.
  • High school diploma required; bachelor's degree preferred.
  • Professional presentation that fits a Class A community environment.

What Separates Strong Candidates Here

The candidates who do well in this type of role understand that occupancy and resident satisfaction aren't separate priorities. They treat follow-up as non-negotiable, not optional. They keep their CRM current because they know a messy pipeline costs leases. And they're equally comfortable walking a vacant unit for a quality check as they are hosting a resident happy hour.

Arlington is a competitive submarket. Prospects have options. The ability to sell a lifestyle and a specific community, not just square footage and amenities, matters here more than it does in markets with less supply pressure. Candidates who've worked lease-up environments or high-traffic urban properties will recognize the pace quickly.

This role is a solid path toward a Community Manager position. The operational exposure you get alongside the leasing responsibility gives you the full picture of how a multifamily property runs, which makes the step up to leading your own site a real possibility with the right performance.

job.title: Community Relations Manager - Cortland National Landing
job.description:
A Sales and Service Hybrid in One of the DC Area's Most Competitive Rental Markets

This role at Cortland's National Landing community in Arlington sits at the intersection of leasing performance and resident experience. It's not a pure leasing consultant position, and it's not a property manager title either. Think of it as the connective tissue between the two: someone who can close a lease in the afternoon and walk a make-ready unit in the morning, then spend the evening responding to a resident concern before it becomes a complaint.

National Landing is the mixed-use district anchored by Amazon HQ2, which means the renter profile here skews toward high-earning professionals with real expectations for service quality. That context matters. Residents in this submarket aren't comparing you to the Class B community down the street. They're comparing you to their last apartment in Seattle or New York. Your ability to deliver a genuinely premium experience, not just talk about one, is what makes or breaks retention.

What the Day-to-Day Actually Looks Like

You'll support the Community Manager in keeping daily operations tight: inspections of vacant units, move-in and move-out walkthroughs, monitoring curb appeal and policy compliance, and managing vendor relationships so work gets done on time and to standard. On the sales side, you're actively working a leasing pipeline. That means disciplined CRM hygiene, prompt follow-up, consultative conversations with prospective residents, and hitting conversion targets consistently.

Resident experience is a real part of the job description, not a tagline. You're planning and executing community events, managing social media presence, and handling resident concerns with professionalism when things go sideways. And they will go sideways sometimes. That's just the job.

  • 2+ years of measurable sales success in a high-volume, short-cycle environment (multifamily, hospitality, luxury retail, or similar)
  • Demonstrated ability to meet or exceed leasing quotas and manage a pipeline with discipline
  • Strong written and verbal communication skills, including the ability to de-escalate difficult resident interactions
  • Comfort with property management software, CRM platforms, and Microsoft Office
  • Experience using social media for community marketing and engagement
  • High school diploma required; bachelor's degree preferred
  • Fair Housing knowledge and general compliance awareness

Where This Role Can Take You

Community Relations Manager is a well-worn path toward an Assistant Community Manager or full Community Manager title at larger operators like Cortland. The skills you build here, specifically pipeline management, operational support, and resident relations at a premium property, transfer cleanly into those next-level roles. Cortland is a vertically integrated operator with a sizable portfolio, which means internal mobility is a real option if you perform and make yourself visible.

If you're someone who gets restless doing only one thing, this role offers genuine variety. The tradeoff is that it demands consistency across all of it. You can't be great at leasing and indifferent to operations. The role only works if you take both halves seriously.

job.title: Community Relations Manager - Cortland on Blake
job.description:
This role has two distinct jobs living inside one title. Half the time you're a leasing consultant, building a pipeline, following up on leads, and closing apartments. The other half you're an operations partner, walking vacant units, running move-in and move-out walkthroughs, keeping vendor relationships on track, and making sure the community looks and runs the way a premium Cortland property is supposed to. If you're only good at one of those things, this probably isn't the right fit.

Cortland on Blake is a Class A community in Denver, and the expectations that come with that label are real. Residents paying premium rents expect concierge-level service, not just a friendly face at the front desk. That means anticipating problems before residents surface them, handling objections without flinching, and running resident events that people actually show up to. It also means your CRM stays clean, your follow-up is disciplined, and your conversion numbers are something you can speak to clearly in a performance review.

On the operational side, you'll work directly alongside the Community Manager or Senior Community Manager to keep daily operations in compliance with Fair Housing guidelines, OSHA standards, and Cortland's own brand requirements. You'll conduct regular property inspections, monitor curb appeal, and flag maintenance needs before they become resident complaints. None of that is glamorous work. It matters anyway.

What separates strong candidates from average ones here is the ability to shift modes without losing momentum. You might spend your morning closing a lease with a prospective resident, spend the afternoon walking a punch list on a make-ready unit, and end the day responding to a vendor concern. People who treat leasing and operations as separate worlds tend to struggle in this structure. People who find both genuinely interesting tend to thrive.

What Cortland is looking for

  • At least 2 years of measurable sales success in a high-velocity environment, multifamily, hospitality, luxury retail, or a comparable field
  • Demonstrated ability to hit and exceed leasing targets, with a clear track record in pipeline management
  • Comfort with property management software, CRM systems, and Microsoft Office
  • Strong written and verbal communication skills, including the ability to handle difficult resident conversations with professionalism
  • Experience with social media as a leasing and engagement tool, not just personal use
  • High school diploma required; a bachelor's degree is preferred but not a dealbreaker

The pay range for this position runs $26 to $30 per hour. Cortland is a vertically integrated operator with in-house design, construction, and management functions, which means there's genuine room to grow within the organization for people who perform. A strong Community Relations Manager here is a natural candidate for a Community Manager role down the line. That path exists, but you'll need to earn it with results.

job.title: Community Relations Manager - Cortland on Pike
job.description:
Sales skill, relationship instinct, and operational discipline. Those three things define whether someone thrives in this role at Cortland on Pike in Arlington, and they're not equally distributed in most candidates. This is not a traditional leasing consultant position with a narrow scope. It sits at the intersection of revenue generation, resident experience, and day-to-day community operations.

The Skills This Role Runs On

Pipeline management is central here. You'll carry a sales pipeline the way a good account executive would, tracking leads, following up with urgency, and closing leases with genuine consultative technique rather than a scripted pitch. Cortland operates at a premium tier, and the residents you're courting at a property like Cortland on Pike expect a buying experience that matches the product. That means reading people quickly, uncovering what actually matters to them, and presenting the community in a way that connects those dots.

On the operational side, you'll work closely with the Community Manager to keep the property running to brand standard. That includes conducting inspections on vacant units, make-ready apartments, and model homes, as well as move-in and move-out walkthroughs. You'll monitor curb appeal, flag maintenance needs, manage vendor relationships, and keep compliance tight across Fair Housing guidelines and OSHA requirements. It's hands-on, detail-oriented work that runs parallel to the leasing activity, not separate from it.

CRM hygiene matters here more than at many properties. Cortland tracks conversion metrics and pipeline health closely, so keeping records accurate and reporting on lead activity isn't optional. If you're someone who treats CRM as an afterthought, this role will expose that quickly.

Resident Experience as a Differentiator

Cortland's brand promise is built on the resident experience, which means your work doesn't stop at lease signing. You'll plan and execute resident events, manage the community's social media presence, and build relationships that drive referrals and retention. The ability to handle resident concerns with empathy and real urgency matters just as much as closing a lease. Residents at a Class A community notice the difference between someone going through the motions and someone who genuinely anticipates what they need.

Arlington's rental market is competitive. Properties in this corridor consistently see strong traffic, but converting that traffic requires more than availability and a nice amenity deck. Residents here are often well-informed, comparison-shopping across multiple premium options, and they respond to professionalism, responsiveness, and a leasing experience that feels consultative rather than transactional.

What Strong Candidates Bring

  • 2 or more years of measurable sales success in a high-velocity, short-cycle environment (multifamily, hospitality, luxury retail, or similar)
  • Demonstrated ability to meet or exceed leasing or sales quotas consistently
  • Comfort with property management software, CRM systems, and Microsoft Office
  • Strong written and verbal communication, including social media content creation
  • A high school diploma or equivalent at minimum; a bachelor's degree is preferred
  • Professional presence that aligns with a premium brand in every interaction

What separates a strong candidate from an average one here is the combination of sales discipline and genuine hospitality instinct. Some people can sell. Some people can build relationships. Cortland needs both in the same person, operating simultaneously, without either one suffering when things get busy.

This role is a natural stepping stone toward a Community Manager position for someone who performs well. The operational exposure you get here, paired with direct revenue accountability, builds exactly the experience that prepares you to run a community independently.

job.title: Community Relations Manager - Cortland Peterson
job.description:
It's 10 a.m. on a Saturday and you've got three prospects touring within the next two hours, a move-in walkthrough at noon, and a resident stopping by the office because their renewal offer "doesn't feel right." That's a pretty normal morning in this role.

Cortland is hiring a Community Relations Manager for their Peterson property in Colorado Springs. The title sits between a leasing consultant and an assistant manager, which is exactly what the job is. You're the face of the community on the sales side, but you're also deep in the operational weeds alongside the Community Manager, covering inspections, vendor coordination, compliance checks, and curb appeal rounds. The role is genuinely dual-track, and candidates who treat it as "just leasing" tend to struggle.

What This Role Actually Covers

On the sales side, you're managing a pipeline with real accountability. That means prompt follow-up, accurate CRM entries, conversion tracking, and closing leases with a consultative approach rather than a pressure-based one. Cortland positions its communities at the upper end of the market, so the expectation is a concierge-level experience from first contact through move-in.

On the operations side, you're helping keep the property running to brand standard. That includes:

  • Regular inspections of vacant units, make-ready apartments, and model homes
  • Move-in and move-out walkthroughs
  • Monitoring for policy violations, maintenance needs, and overall property condition
  • Managing vendor relationships and following up on project timelines
  • Supporting Fair Housing compliance and OSHA safety requirements
  • Running resident events and managing the community's social media presence

Pay runs $26 to $30 per hour depending on experience.

What Cortland Is Looking For

Two or more years of measurable sales performance is the baseline. Multifamily experience is relevant, but Cortland also values backgrounds in hospitality, luxury retail, or premium automotive because the selling style translates. A high school diploma is required; a bachelor's degree is preferred.

Beyond the resume, strong candidates here tend to be organized under pressure, comfortable in property management software and CRM platforms, and genuinely good at de-escalating resident concerns without passing the problem up the chain every time. Written communication matters too, since you'll be managing social content and responding to leads in writing regularly.

One honest note: this is a high-visibility role where your numbers are tracked closely. Conversion rates, pipeline health, and leasing KPIs will be reviewed consistently. If that kind of accountability energizes you, the role fits well. If it feels like surveillance, it probably won't be a great match. Cortland runs a performance-oriented culture, and that's not a criticism, it's just useful context before you apply.

For someone with leasing and light operational experience who wants a clear path toward a full Community Manager seat, this is a reasonable next step. You'll get exposure to the full scope of onsite operations, not just traffic and tours.

job.title: Community Relations Manager- Cortland Whitehall
job.description:
Sales, Service, and Site Operations at a Recognized Multifamily Operator

Community Relations Manager roles at large, vertically integrated multifamily companies carry more operational weight than the title suggests. You're not simply a leasing agent with a fancier name. At a property like Cortland Whitehall in Charlotte, you're functioning as both the revenue engine and a direct support partner to the Community Manager, which means your day spans consultative sales conversations in the morning and compliance walkthroughs in the afternoon.

Cortland operates its own design, construction, and management functions in-house, which shapes how this role works in practice. You're not waiting on a third-party management company to interpret standards. Expectations around brand presentation, curb appeal, and resident experience come from a single, consistent source, and you're accountable to those standards directly.

What This Role Actually Involves

On the leasing side, you'll manage a full sales pipeline: initial outreach, timely follow-up, CRM documentation, and closing. Cortland emphasizes conversion rates and KPIs, so you'll need to be disciplined about pipeline hygiene, not just personable at the front desk. Consultative selling is the expectation here. That means uncovering what a prospect actually needs and connecting it to what the community offers, rather than running through a script.

Operationally, you'll conduct inspections of vacant units, make-ready apartments, and model homes. You'll handle move-in and move-out walkthroughs, monitor for policy compliance and maintenance needs, and manage vendor relationships to keep projects on schedule. You'll also own the community's social media presence and plan resident events designed to build retention and referrals.

  • 2 or more years of measurable sales success in high-velocity, short-cycle environments (multifamily, hospitality, luxury retail, or similar)
  • Demonstrated ability to meet or exceed leasing quotas and conversion targets
  • Experience with property management software, CRM platforms, and Microsoft Office
  • Strong written and verbal communication skills, with the ability to resolve resident concerns professionally
  • High school diploma required; bachelor's degree preferred
  • Familiarity with Fair Housing guidelines and OSHA safety standards

Where Strong Candidates Separate Themselves

The candidates who thrive in this role don't treat leasing and operations as separate jobs. They understand that a tight make-ready process protects occupancy, that accurate CRM records make follow-up faster, and that resident events directly affect renewal rates. That connecting-the-dots thinking is what distinguishes a high performer here from someone who's simply good at closing leases.

This role also builds a specific skill set that transfers well. Experience managing a sales pipeline at a Class A community, combined with hands-on operational exposure, positions you well for a Community Manager role. You'll come out of this position with documented KPI performance, vendor management experience, and a working knowledge of compliance processes, all of which matter when you're ready to run a site independently.

Charlotte's multifamily market remains one of the more competitive in the Southeast, with consistent renter demand and a growing supply of new product. Holding occupancy and sustaining NOI at a Cortland asset in that environment requires someone who treats every prospect interaction as a real sales opportunity, not just a tour.

job.title: Concierge - Cortland Mount Vernon
job.description:
What You'll Actually Do

The Concierge role at Cortland Mount Vernon in Washington, DC is the operational center of the resident experience. You're the first person residents see in the morning and the last one they interact with before heading out. That means your days include managing front desk activity, greeting residents and guests, coordinating with maintenance and cleaning crews, and fielding everything from package questions to amenity reservations. You'll issue and track building access cards and parking credentials, keep the lobby and shared spaces presentable, and make sure opening and closing procedures happen without a hitch. When a resident event is on the calendar, you're part of the coordination effort. When a work order needs to move, you're helping it along.

This isn't a passive role. You're communicating constantly across leasing, management, maintenance, and vendor teams. Strong days look like smooth handoffs and small problems resolved before residents notice them. Tougher days mean handling competing priorities at the front desk while keeping the lobby calm and professional.

What You Bring

  • At least 3 years in a customer-facing role, ideally in residential property management, hospitality, or a similar front-desk environment
  • Comfort with package and mail handling across carriers like USPS, FedEx, and UPS
  • Solid working knowledge of Microsoft Office and general office technology
  • Strong organizational habits and the ability to stay composed when multiple things need attention at once
  • High school diploma or equivalent required
  • Ability to lift items up to 20 pounds as part of daily duties

What separates strong candidates from average ones here is genuine situational awareness. You notice when the lobby supply station needs restocking before a resident points it out. You remember a resident's name by their second visit. You communicate a maintenance delay to a resident before they come back to ask about it. That kind of proactive attention is the difference between doing the job and doing it well.

Where This Role Can Take You

Concierge is a real entry point into multifamily operations at a company the scale of Cortland, which operates as a vertically integrated owner-operator with in-house design, construction, and management. People who perform well in this seat often move into leasing consultant roles, assistant community manager positions, or resident experience coordinator functions. The skills you build here, including resident communication, vendor coordination, and operational follow-through, transfer directly into any site-level property management role. Cortland's size means internal mobility is genuinely possible, and starting in a resident-facing position gives you a strong foundation in what actually drives occupancy and retention at the property level.

job.title: Floating Leasing & Sales Consultant
job.description:
Consultative selling and pipeline discipline are the core skills this role runs on. Not just friendly conversations with prospective residents, but structured follow-up, conversion tracking, and the ability to read where a lead sits in the funnel and move them forward. Cortland is hiring a Floating Leasing and Sales Consultant to cover multiple communities across the Denver market, which means your adaptability gets tested constantly. Different floor plans, different price points, different competitive sets, sometimes all in the same week.

What the Work Actually Looks Like

You're managing a live sales pipeline every day. That means logging interactions in the CRM promptly, following up before leads go cold, and hitting conversion targets that matter to the community's occupancy and revenue performance. Cortland measures results, so you'll know exactly where you stand. When a prospect walks in or calls in, you're not reciting amenities from a sheet. You're asking questions, identifying what they actually need, and presenting the community in a way that connects with their specific situation.

Beyond closing leases, you'll also contribute to marketing programs and social media presence for the communities you support, and you'll help plan resident events that build the kind of loyalty that keeps renewals strong. Objection handling is part of the daily rhythm. Price, timing, floor plan availability. You'll get good at turning those moments into conversations instead of dead ends.

Because this is a floating role, you won't have one home base. That's a real distinction from a standard leasing consultant position. You need to get up to speed on a new property quickly, understand its competitive positioning, and represent it confidently without weeks of ramp time. That skill, absorbing a new asset fast, is genuinely useful if you eventually move into a property manager role where you'd inherit a community you didn't lease up yourself.

Skills That Drive Performance Here

  • Consultative sales ability: uncovering needs, not just pitching features
  • CRM discipline: accurate records, consistent follow-up, no leads falling through the cracks
  • Adaptability across multiple properties with different resident profiles and price points
  • Comfort being measured against goals, KPIs, and conversion metrics on a regular basis
  • Strong personal presentation that holds up in a premium Class A environment
  • Coachability: Cortland operates with clear expectations and gives feedback; you need to act on it

Why Cortland, and Why This Role Specifically

Cortland is vertically integrated, which means in-house design, construction, and operations all sit under one roof. For a leasing consultant, that matters because you're selling a product the company controls end to end. When a resident has a concern about a unit finish or a maintenance issue, you're backed by an organization that can actually act on it. That's a different conversation than leasing for a third-party management company.

The floating structure also builds a kind of market fluency that a single-property role doesn't. After six months of covering multiple Denver communities, you'll understand traffic patterns, competitive concessions, and pricing dynamics across a real cross-section of the portfolio. That's a strong foundation if you want to move toward a leasing manager or assistant property manager track. Pay for this role falls in the $24 to $26 per hour range.

Six months of sales or customer-facing experience is the floor for this role. What actually separates strong candidates is follow-up discipline. Most people can do a good tour. Fewer people maintain consistent, timely outreach through a full sales cycle without letting warm leads go cold.

job.title: Front Door Waste Technician - Cortland Belmar
job.description:
Valet trash collection isn't a glamorous role, and anyone who tells you otherwise hasn't done it. You're working evenings, climbing stairs, hauling bags in Denver winters, and dealing with residents who occasionally leave things outside their door that they absolutely should not. That's the honest version. It's also steady, physical work with a clear purpose: keeping a well-maintained Cortland community clean and running the way residents expect it to.

What the Work Actually Looks Like

This position supports the Front Door Valet Trash Program at Cortland Belmar, a multifamily community in the Denver metro area. Your shift involves walking the property, collecting waste bags from resident doorsteps, transferring them to a designated vehicle, and disposing of everything properly onsite. You'll also clear common areas including pool zones, dog relief stations, mail kiosks, and parking lots.

Beyond the collection itself, there's a documentation component. You'll use a mobile app to log your start and end times, photograph issues, and report anything that falls outside normal conditions. Spills get cleaned. Compactor areas get cleared. Safety standards don't get skipped. This role requires someone who follows a process consistently, not just when it's convenient.

  • Collect and transport waste and recycling from resident doorsteps each shift
  • Clean spills, compactor areas, and debris from dumpster zones as needed
  • Remove trash from common areas and replace liners at dog pods, poolside, and mail kiosks
  • Log service activity and document issues using a smartphone app
  • Operate a vehicle to move between areas of the property; a valid Class E driver's license is required
  • Lift up to 50 pounds repeatedly and climb multiple flights of stairs throughout each shift
  • Work day, evening, and weekend hours as the schedule requires

What You Bring

A high school diploma or equivalent is preferred, and a year of experience in waste collection or a service-oriented role gives you a real leg up. You need to own a smartphone with an active data plan since the app is central to how this role gets tracked and managed. Familiarity with EPA and OSHA safety regulations matters here. Bilingual candidates are a plus given the diverse resident populations at larger multifamily communities.

The candidates who thrive in this kind of role aren't necessarily the ones with the most experience. They're the ones who show up on time in bad weather, run their route without cutting corners, and communicate clearly when something's off. Consistency is the skill that actually counts.

Context Worth Knowing

Cortland is a vertically integrated multifamily operator, meaning they handle design, construction, and management in-house across a large national portfolio. Belmar is a real community with residents who notice when things are done well and when they're not. Valet trash has become a standard amenity at Class A properties, and the person doing this job directly shapes how residents feel about where they live. That's not a small thing.

The pay range for this role is $19 to $20 per hour.

job.title: Front Door Waste Technician - Cortland Phillips Creek Ranch
job.description:
Valet trash is one of those amenity services that residents notice immediately when it goes wrong and barely think about when it goes right. That's the standard you're working toward in this role at Cortland's Phillips Creek Ranch community in Frisco, TX. Consistency, reliability, and a clean property every single morning.

As a Front Door Waste Technician, you're running Cortland's valet trash program at a multifamily community. The work is physical and route-based. You'll collect bagged waste from resident doorsteps, transfer it into designated containers, and transport it to the onsite compactor. You're also responsible for clearing loose trash from common areas, pool decks, dog stations, and mail kiosks, and you'll do a lot of stair climbing to get there. Shifts can run days, evenings, or weekends, and you'll be working outdoors regardless of weather. If you want a desk job, this isn't it.

Cortland is a top-10 multifamily owner and operator in the country, and their properties run at a high standard. Phillips Creek Ranch is a well-maintained community in a competitive Frisco submarket where resident experience directly affects retention. The valet trash program is part of that experience. When the service runs clean and on time, it supports occupancy. When it doesn't, leasing hears about it. You're a visible part of the operation even if most residents never know your name.

The job requires you to use a mobile app to log start and end times, report issues, and document your route with photos during each shift. You'll need a smartphone that can run it and a data plan. A valid Class E driver's license is required since you'll be operating a vehicle on and around the property.

What Cortland is Looking For

  • High school diploma or equivalent preferred
  • At least one year in waste services, facilities, or a service-oriented environment
  • Working knowledge of EPA and OSHA guidelines
  • Ability to lift up to 50 pounds and climb stairs repeatedly during a shift
  • Comfort working outdoors in variable weather conditions
  • Strong communication skills with residents and property staff
  • Valid Class E driver's license and a smartphone with an active data plan
  • Bilingual English/Spanish is a plus in this market

From a career standpoint, this role builds skills that transfer directly into broader property maintenance. Technicians who demonstrate reliability and attention to detail in service roles like this one often move into make-ready, grounds, or general maintenance positions over time. You're learning how a multifamily property operates from the ground up, literally. Cortland's vertical integration means there's real infrastructure behind the company, and internal movement is a realistic path for people who perform.

The honest reality: this job involves unfriendly odors, early or late hours depending on your schedule, and physical repetition. People who thrive here aren't bothered by any of that. They take ownership of their route, notice what's out of place, and take pride in handing off a clean property at the end of every shift.

job.title: Front Door Waste Technician - Cortland Phillips Creek Ranch
job.description:
It's 8 p.m. on a Tuesday, and residents at a Frisco apartment community have set their trash bags outside their front doors. Your job is to move through the property efficiently, collect every bag, and leave every doorstep clean before the night is done. That's the core of this role.

What This Role Actually Involves

Cortland is hiring a Front Door Waste Technician for Phillips Creek Ranch, a multifamily community in Frisco, TX. This is a physical, route-based position. You'll walk the property, collect waste bags from residents' doorsteps, transfer them into a designated vehicle or hamper, and deposit everything into onsite waste containers or compactors. You'll also clean up spills around compactor and dumpster areas, clear loose trash from sidewalks and parking lots, service dog waste stations, and keep common areas like pool decks and mail kiosks clean.

Cortland uses a mobile app to track this work. You'll log your start and end times, report issues, and take photos during your shift. That means you need a smartphone with a data plan and a current, valid driver's license since you may drive between locations.

The physical demands are real. Expect to climb multiple flights of stairs repeatedly, lift bags weighing up to 50 pounds, and work in outdoor conditions year-round. Frisco summers are hot and humid. This isn't a desk job, and the schedule includes evenings and weekends.

What Cortland Is Looking For

  • High school diploma or equivalent (preferred)
  • At least one year of experience in waste management, janitorial, or a service-oriented field
  • Familiarity with EPA and OSHA safety regulations
  • Valid Class E driver's license
  • Smartphone (Android 2.2 or iPhone iOS 4.3 or newer) with active data plan
  • Ability to communicate professionally with residents and staff
  • Bilingual English/Spanish is a plus
  • Comfortable working in variable weather and around unpleasant odors

What separates a strong candidate here isn't just physical ability. It's consistency. Routes need to be completed fully and on time, every shift. Residents notice when a doorstep gets missed, and in a Class A community like Phillips Creek Ranch, that kind of detail directly affects resident satisfaction scores. Someone who takes pride in a clean property and doesn't cut corners on a quiet Tuesday night is exactly who this role needs.

Cortland is a vertically integrated multifamily operator, one of the ten largest owners and operators in the country. They manage their own properties rather than relying on third-party management, which means standards and accountability flow directly from ownership. For someone interested in growing within property operations, this type of ground-level role builds familiarity with how a well-run community functions from the outside in.

job.title: Front Door Waste Technician - Cortland Sunbury Ridge
job.description:
Cortland runs one of the larger vertically integrated multifamily portfolios in the country, and keeping a community's curb appeal and sanitation program running cleanly is a direct input to resident satisfaction scores and renewal rates. This role sits at the front lines of that daily operation at Sunbury Ridge in Columbus.

What the Work Actually Looks Like

The Front Door Waste Technician position runs Cortland's valet trash program at the community level. Each shift, you're moving through the property collecting waste bags from residents' doorsteps, loading them into a designated vehicle, and disposing of everything into on-site containers or compactors. That sounds straightforward, but the physical demands are real: you're climbing stairs, covering walking distances across the property, lifting bags up to 50 pounds, and doing it in whatever Columbus weather decides to show up that day.

Beyond the collection route itself, the job includes:

  • Cleaning up spills and loose trash around dumpsters, compactors, and common areas like the pool deck, dog stations, and mail kiosks
  • Logging start and end times and reporting issues through a smartphone app during every shift
  • Operating the compactor packing mechanism when applicable and keeping that area clear of debris
  • Replacing trash liners in common area receptacles and maintaining the overall cleanliness of the grounds
  • Interacting with residents and on-site staff in a professional, approachable way throughout the route

You'll need a valid Class E driver's license and your own smartphone with a current data plan. Shifts can run days, evenings, or weekends, so schedule flexibility matters here.

What Cortland Looks For

A high school diploma or equivalent is preferred, along with some experience in waste management, janitorial work, or a service-oriented environment. Familiarity with EPA and OSHA guidelines is a plus. Bilingual candidates are welcome. The bigger filter is reliability and attention to detail: routes that get skipped or done halfway show up quickly in resident complaints, and at a company that tracks resident experience carefully, that feedback matters.

The Bigger Picture

Cortland was founded in 2005 and has grown into a top-10 multifamily owner and operator in the US, with in-house design, construction, and management functions all under one roof. The resident-centric model means ground-level roles like this one carry more operational weight than they might at a third-party managed community. Consistent, professional valet service is one of the amenity touchpoints residents notice most, and it shows up in reviews and retention numbers. If you're someone who takes pride in leaving a space cleaner than you found it and wants to work for an organization that actually tracks outcomes, this is a solid place to do that work.

job.title: Groundskeeper
job.description:
Grounds work is physical, repetitive, and detail-driven. It rewards people who notice things: a piece of trash near the entrance that everyone else walked past, a drainage issue that'll become a headache if it's ignored for another week, a bed that needs edging before the weekend. That attentiveness is the core skill this role runs on, and Cortland is looking for someone who brings it every day at their Blacklick, OH community.

What the Work Actually Looks Like

Your days center on keeping the property's exterior in consistently good shape. That means daily trash and debris removal across the grounds, interior and exterior cleaning of common building areas, and seasonal tasks that shift with the weather (think snow, leaves, heat-related turf stress). You'll also step in to support the broader maintenance team when resident needs arise or when community events require extra hands.

The honest version: this job has early mornings and outdoor conditions that don't always cooperate. You're the first line of impression for prospects driving through and residents coming home from work. The property's curb appeal is largely yours to own, which is real responsibility without a lot of fanfare attached to it.

Skills That Carry This Role

  • Attention to detail at the property-wide level, not just your immediate task
  • Physical stamina for outdoor work across all seasons
  • Basic communication skills for coordinating with maintenance staff and responding to resident interactions professionally
  • Self-direction: you're often working without direct supervision and need to prioritize without being told

Grounds experience helps, but it's not the only path in. People who've worked in landscaping, facilities, custodial services, or even warehouse environments often transition well because the habits transfer: showing up consistently, working clean, finishing what you start.

What Cortland Brings to the Table

Cortland is a vertically integrated operator with a large national portfolio, which means their internal training infrastructure is more developed than you'd find at a smaller management company. Groundskeepers here can earn CPO, EPA, and CPR certifications through in-house proctors, not third-party courses you have to chase down yourself. That's a genuine career asset. Compensation includes monthly commission and quarterly bonuses alongside full benefits, and they offer individual growth plans for people who want to move into maintenance tech or broader property operations roles over time.

For someone who wants to stay in groundskeeping long-term, the structure supports that. For someone who sees this as an entry point into the broader maintenance side of property management, the certification pathway and mentorship access make the trajectory real.

job.title: Groundskeeper - Cortland Sugar Land - Houston, TX
job.description:
What You'll Actually Do

The Cortland Sugar Land community lives and dies by its curb appeal, and this role sits at the center of that. Every day, you're walking the property, picking up trash and debris, cleaning interior and exterior common areas, and making sure nothing slips through the cracks before a resident or prospect notices it. You're not just maintaining a checklist. You're the first set of eyes on the property each morning, and what you catch early keeps bigger problems from growing.

Beyond daily upkeep, you'll handle seasonal cleaning tasks and pitch in on maintenance requests and resident functions as they come up. That cross-functional piece matters more than it sounds. Groundskeepers who pay attention during those moments pick up skills that move them into maintenance tech roles faster than people expect.

What You Bring

  • A consistent, detail-oriented approach to physical work outdoors and in common areas
  • The ability to stay organized across a large property footprint without needing constant direction
  • Reliability. Showing up on time and completing your route matters here more than almost anything else.
  • A willingness to earn certifications. Cortland trains in-house for CPO, EPA, and CPR through certified proctors, so you don't need these on day one, but you need to take them seriously once you're here.

Where This Role Can Take You

Groundskeeper and porter positions at well-resourced operators like Cortland are genuinely underrated entry points into property management careers. The in-house training structure here gives you access to certifications that carry real weight when you move toward a maintenance technician or lead tech role. If you're early in your property management career and you want hands-on exposure to how a Class A multifamily community actually runs day to day, this is a practical way in. You'll see make-readies, watch turns happen, and understand the operational rhythm of a large residential property in ways that classroom training simply can't replicate.

Cortland is a vertically integrated operator, meaning design, construction, and management all sit under one roof. That structure creates more internal mobility than you'd find at a typical third-party management company. The individual growth plan they offer isn't filler language. For someone who shows up, earns their certifications, and builds relationships with the maintenance team, a clear path forward exists.

The Sugar Land submarket sits in the southwest Houston corridor, a dense and growing area with strong multifamily demand. Working on a well-maintained Cortland asset in this location means you're learning standards that transfer across the portfolio.

job.title: Housekepper
job.description:
Cortland runs a tight operation. One of the top 10 multifamily owners in the country, they build, design, and manage their communities in-house, which means the people on the ground at each property actually matter to how the portfolio performs. This Housekeeper role at their Pearland, TX community is a direct part of that.

The job is straightforward in scope. You're responsible for keeping common areas clean, presentable, and consistently maintained. That means fitness centers, community rooms, laundry rooms, restrooms, office spaces, and any other shared areas. Dusting, vacuuming, sweeping, trash removal, restocking supplies. You also keep your equipment and storage area organized, and when you spot something that looks like a maintenance issue, you flag it. That last part matters more than it sounds. A housekeeper who notices a slow leak or a broken fixture and reports it early can prevent a work order from turning into a capital expense. Good eyes are genuinely useful here.

What makes Cortland's setup worth paying attention to is the training infrastructure. They've built a mobile training unit, an actual 18-wheeler that deploys to your city, covering HVAC basics, plumbing, electrical, fire safety, and pool equipment. For someone starting in a housekeeping role who wants to eventually move into maintenance tech or facilities work, that's real access. They also offer in-house CPO, EPA, and CPR certification through proctors on staff. These aren't online checkbox courses. You come out with credentials that transfer.

The compensation includes a monthly commission component and quarterly bonuses alongside full benefits. Certain communities also operate on a 4-day work week, though you'd want to confirm whether the Pearland property is structured that way.

What they're looking for

  • Reliable, detail-oriented approach to cleaning and sanitation standards
  • Ability to manage cleaning supply inventory and storage independently
  • Comfort working across multiple common area types within a multifamily community
  • Willingness to observe and report maintenance concerns as they come up
  • Someone who takes pride in the physical condition of the property, not just the checklist

The candidates who stand out in roles like this aren't necessarily the ones with the longest resume. They're the ones who treat resident-facing spaces with real care, communicate clearly when something's off, and show up consistently. In a vertically integrated company like Cortland, a strong housekeeper who's curious about the building's systems has a visible path toward maintenance roles over time. That ladder is real here, and the training resources to climb it are already in place.

job.title: Leasing Hub Associate, Bilingual - Sandy Springs, GA
job.description:
Picture this: it's 10 a.m. and you've already handled a dozen inbound leads through RealPage, sent follow-up texts to prospects who toured last weekend, and booked four new appointments for communities across the Atlanta metro. You haven't set foot on a single property. That's the job.

What This Role Actually Is

Cortland is one of the larger vertically integrated multifamily operators in the country, and this position sits inside their centralized leasing hub. You're not stationed at a single community greeting walk-ins. You're working the top of the funnel: fielding calls and digital inquiries, qualifying prospects, and handing off warm leads to onsite leasing teams. Think of yourself as the first voice a future resident hears before they ever step into a model unit.

The volume here is real. You're managing 40 to 50 leads per day across multiple communities, which means your ability to stay organized and keep conversations genuine under that pace matters more than a polished script. Cortland uses RealPage's OneSite platform, so comfort there is expected. L2L experience is a plus worth mentioning on your resume if you have it.

Bilingual fluency is a core requirement for this particular opening, not a nice-to-have. Spanish-speaking prospects make up a meaningful share of the traffic Cortland fields in their Southeast markets, and being able to hold a full leasing conversation in both languages is what makes you effective in this seat.

Who Fits Here

  • Two or more years in a customer-facing sales or service role, ideally one where you were measured on conversion or volume

job.title: Leasing & Sales Consultant
job.description:
It's a Tuesday afternoon and you've got three tours back-to-back, two pending applications waiting on documents, and a prospect from last week who went quiet sitting in your CRM with a follow-up overdue. That's the rhythm of this role. Organized, social, and sales-driven in equal measure.

What This Role Actually Is

Cortland is one of the larger vertically integrated multifamily operators in the country, and this Leasing and Sales Consultant position sits at the front line of everything they've built. You're responsible for occupancy and revenue at your community. That means working a real sales pipeline, not just showing units and hoping people sign. You'll track leads in a CRM, manage follow-up cadences, hit conversion targets, and apply consultative techniques to match each prospect to the right home rather than just the first available one.

Beyond leasing, you'll carry the resident experience through the full lifecycle. You'll anticipate needs, handle objections without flinching, and build the kind of relationships that generate referrals. Cortland properties operate at a premium standard, so your personal presentation and how you represent the brand in every interaction matters more here than it might at a Class B or C community. You'll also manage social media presence and resident events, which means this role has a creative, outward-facing dimension that pure leasing roles often don't.

Expect coaching. Cortland tracks KPIs closely, and feedback is part of how they develop their leasing teams. That's not a warning; it's actually useful for people who want to grow. Strong performers at this level often move into senior leasing roles, assistant manager positions, or eventually property management. The pipeline from leasing consultant to manager is one of the most well-worn career tracks in this industry, and companies like Cortland tend to promote from within when occupancy and conversion numbers back it up.

What They're Looking For

  • At least 6 months of sales, customer service, or performance-oriented experience. Apartment leasing background is a plus but not required.
  • Comfort being held to goals. This is a metrics-tracked role and the expectation is that you hit your numbers consistently.
  • CRM proficiency or the willingness to learn quickly. Sloppy pipeline management is one of the fastest ways to underperform in a leasing role.
  • Genuine ability to build rapport across a wide range of prospects, not just the easy conversations.
  • Coachable mindset. If you shut down when a manager gives you direct feedback, this won't be a comfortable fit.
  • Professional presentation that matches a premium brand environment.

One thing that separates strong leasing consultants from average ones at communities like this: the ability to follow up without being prompted. Most lost leases die in the silence between a tour and a decision. If you're the type who builds a system for staying in touch rather than waiting for prospects to call back, you'll stand out here.

job.title: Leasing & Sales Consultant
job.description:
Leasing is a sales job. That sounds obvious, but a lot of consultants in this industry treat it like a hospitality job and wonder why their conversion numbers stay flat. Cortland built their leasing program around the opposite philosophy: pipeline discipline, consultative selling, and measurable close rates. If you've got a knack for reading people quickly and you actually enjoy the chase of turning a cold lead warm, that's the core skill set this role runs on.

What This Role Actually Demands

Your days center on traffic and conversion. You're working leads through a CRM, following up consistently (which sounds easy and isn't), and running tours that do more than walk someone through a floorplan. Consultative selling means you're asking questions before you start pitching. What's your commute situation? What did you hate about your last place? That information shapes how you present the community, and it's what separates a strong leasing consultant from someone who just hands out brochures and hopes for the best.

Beyond the sales mechanics, you're also managing the community's social presence and planning resident events. It's a wider scope than most leasing roles. Cortland positions their communities as a lifestyle product, so your job isn't just filling units. It's maintaining the brand experience before, during, and after the lease is signed.

The Skillset That Carries You Here

  • Pipeline management and disciplined follow-up. Letting leads go cold is the most common way leasing consultants miss their goals.
  • Objection handling. Rent too high, amenities not quite right, timing feels off. You need to work through those conversations without flinching.
  • CRM accuracy. Cortland tracks lead activity and conversion metrics closely. Sloppy records hurt your numbers and your credibility.
  • Adaptability to coaching. Performance-focused operators give a lot of feedback. The consultants who grow are the ones who implement it quickly.
  • Six or more months in a sales or customer-facing role is preferred, though the right attitude and coachability can carry someone with less.

Why Cortland Specifically

Cortland is vertically integrated, which matters more than it sounds on paper. Design, construction, and operations all sit under one roof, which means the product is intentional and the brand standards are consistent across their portfolio. You're not selling a commodity apartment. That's a real advantage when you're trying to justify a premium price point to a prospect who's also touring the Class B community down the street.

One thing worth knowing: leasing consultants at operators like Cortland tend to build skills that travel well. Strong CRM habits, proven conversion metrics, and experience with a recognized national brand make it a credible stepping stone into an assistant manager role if that's where you want to go. Not every company gives leasing staff that kind of visibility into the broader business. Cortland's structure means you're closer to the operation than you'd be at a smaller fee management shop.

This position is based in Davenport, FL, which sits in the broader Orlando market. That metro has seen consistent demand and significant new supply over the past few years, so competition for renters is real. Your ability to differentiate the community on tour, not just on price, will matter.
